docs.title: REACH docs
docs.text: Use this page as the documentation entry point.

docs.title: New here
docs.text:
• getting-started.md - install, run, and verify your first callback flow
  • architecture.md - understand how public, admin, DNS, and DB fit together

docs.title: Operate REACH
docs.text:
• servers.md - start public/admin roles and protocol listeners
  • dns.md - run DNS and manage zones from DB/admin API
  • logs.md - stream and filter request logs
  • config.md - env and preset precedence, multi-host setup

docs.title: Payloads and rules
docs.text:
• forge.md - generate payloads and create dynamic routes
  • ifttt_rules.md - rule matching, templates, actions, chaining
  • ifttt_builder.html - visual IFTTT rule builder

docs.title: CLI reference
docs.text: • cli/index.md - command map and command-specific examples

docs.title: Deploy and support
docs.text:
• deployment.md - local, multi-host, Docker deployment patterns
  • troubleshooting.md - common issues and direct fixes

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: REACH is split into distinct runtime surfaces so operators can expose only what is needed.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
• public server handles callback traffic and dynamic route responses
  • admin server exposes management APIs (routes, logs, rules, DNS zones)
  • dns service provides authoritative OOB callbacks and DNS logging
  • database stores routes, logs, and zone/rule state

docs.title: Data flow
docs.text:
Inbound traffic (HTTP/FTP/WSS/DNS)
        |
        v
Protocol handler -> route/rule resolution -> response
        |
        v
Shared logging -> database -> admin API -> CLI tail/queries

docs.title: Process layout
docs.text:
• Single-host: run role=both + DNS as separate process.
  • Multi-host: run public/admin/DNS independently against the same shared DB.

docs.title: Why this split matters
docs.text:
• Limits exposure: admin API can stay internal.
  • Keeps OOB DNS independent from HTTP listener lifecycle.
  • Enables horizontal or host-level separation without changing user workflows.

docs.title: Related docs
docs.text:
• servers.md
  • dns.md
  • config.md
  • logs.md

docs.title: Configuration and deployment
docs.text: REACH loads config in this order: CLI flags > preset > env/.env > defaults.

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text:
• REACH_DB_URL - SQLAlchemy URL (use Postgres for multi-host)
  • REACH_DB_FILE - SQLite file path (used when no DB URL)
  • REACH_DB_ECHO=1 - SQLAlchemy echo
  • REACH_FORGE_PLUGIN_PATHS - extra plugin paths

docs.title: Presets
docs.text:
Presets define server layout:

{
  "server": {
    "http": { "host": "0.0.0.0", "port": 9000 },
    "ftp":  { "host": "0.0.0.0", "port": 2121 },
    "wss":  { "host": "0.0.0.0", "port": 8443 },
    "role": "both",
    "log_level": "info",
    "protocol": "http"
  },
  "admin": { "host": "0.0.0.0", "port": 8001 },
  "db": {
    "url": "sqlite:///./reach_core.db",
    "echo": false
  }
}

Run:

reach server start --preset ./my-preset.json

docs.title: Multi-host deployment (Postgres)
docs.text:
Each service can run on its own host/IP. All of them must point to the same DB.

Example env:

REACH_DB_URL=postgresql://user:pass@DB_IP:5432/reach

Admin host:

reach server start --role admin --host ADMIN_IP --port 8001

Public HTTP host:

reach server start --role public --host PUBLIC_IP --port 8000

DNS host:

reach dns serve --host DNS_IP --port 53 --db-zones

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: Use the smallest deployment shape that fits your engagement.

docs.title: 1. Local single-host (recommended for development)
docs.text:
reach server start --role both --port 8000
reach dns serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 53 --db-zones

Good for feature testing and low-friction local demos.

docs.title: 2. Multi-host (recommended for team/engagement use)
docs.text:
All services point to the same DB:

REACH_DB_URL=postgresql://user:pass@DB_IP:5432/reach

Example split: - admin host: reach server start --role admin --host ADMIN_IP --port 8001 - public host: reach server start --role public --host PUBLIC_IP --port 8000 - dns host: reach dns serve --host DNS_IP --port 53 --db-zones

This setup improves isolation and allows separate network controls.

docs.title: 3. Dockerized core server
docs.text:
The server command supports Docker execution:

reach server start --role both --docker --image reach:local

Common flags: - --rebuild rebuild image before run - --name set container name - --dockerfile choose Dockerfile path - --context choose build context - --detach/--no-detach run background or foreground

docs.title: Security baseline
docs.text:
• Bind admin to private interfaces only.
  • Use strict firewall rules around DNS and admin ports.
  • Use short-lived infrastructure and engagement-specific domains.
  • Keep explicit authorization artifacts for every deployment.

docs.title: Related docs
docs.text:
• config.md
  • servers.md
  • dns.md

docs.title: DNS service
docs.text: The DNS service is a separate process used for OOB callbacks and query logging. It integrates with the core database for live zone management.

docs.title: Run
docs.text: reach dns serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 53 --db-zones

docs.title: Wildcard vs strict
docs.text:
• Default: wildcard mode (any in-zone name answers A/AAAA)
  • --strict-zone: only the zone apex answers A/AAAA

docs.title: DB-backed zones (recommended)
docs.text:
Zones are stored in the core database and managed via the admin API: - GET /api/dns/zones - POST /api/dns/zones - PATCH /api/dns/zones/{id} - DELETE /api/dns/zones/{id}

The DNS service refreshes zones periodically: - --zones-refresh 2.0 (seconds)

docs.title: Static zone (optional)
docs.text:
You can still provide a single static zone:

reach dns serve --domain oob.example.com --a 203.0.113.10

docs.title: Daemon mode
docs.text:
Run DNS in the background:

reach dns serve --daemon --pidfile /tmp/reach-dns.pid

docs.title: Forge (payload generation)
docs.text:
Forge creates payloads and can register them as dynamic routes via the admin API.

Examples:

reach forge --list
reach forge payload new xss_basic --endpoint /xss --payload-kwarg callback_url=http://127.0.0.1:9000/beacon

Plugins: - Place custom generators under forge_plugins/<family>/<name>.py - Modules exposing generate(**kwargs) are auto-registered

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text: This guide gets you from clone to first callback capture quickly.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.12+
  • Local terminal with network access to your chosen ports

docs.title: Install
docs.text: python -m pip install -e .

docs.title: Start core services
docs.text:
Run public and admin together:

reach server start --role both --port 8000

With --role both, public traffic is served on 8000 and admin API defaults to 8001.

docs.title: Create a test payload route
docs.text:
In a second terminal:

reach forge payload new xss_basic \
  --endpoint /xss \
  --payload-kwarg callback_url=http://127.0.0.1:8000/beacon \
  --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001

docs.title: Observe requests
docs.text:
In a third terminal:

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001

Request your route:

curl -i http://127.0.0.1:8000/xss

You should see a new log entry in the tail output.

docs.title: Optional: start DNS for OOB
docs.text: reach dns serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 53 --db-zones

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Read architecture.md for system behavior and boundaries.
  • Read cli/index.md for command-level details.
  • Read deployment.md before exposing REACH outside local development.

docs.title: IFTTT Rules
docs.text: This document describes the IFTTT rule system, how rules are evaluated, and the supported match/action parameters.

docs.title: Evaluation model
docs.text:
• Rules are evaluated in priority order (lowest priority first, then lowest id).
  • Default behavior is post-route: the rule only applies if a dynamic route exists.
  • Rules can explicitly set match.stage: "pre" to run before route lookup.
  • If a rule has action.create_route, it can still match without an existing route and will create the route for future requests.

docs.title: Rule object
docs.text: Each rule is a JSON object with: - name (string) - enabled (bool, default: true) - priority (int, default: 100) - match (object) - action (object)

docs.title: Match parameters
docs.text:
All match values are regexes (Python re.search). Use ^...$ for exact matches.

Leaf fields: - method (string) - HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) - path (string) - request path, e.g. /callback - host (string) - client_ip (string) - body (string) - request body as text - headers (object) - map of header name -> regex, header names are case-insensitive - query (object) - map of query param -> regex - state_key (string, template) - resolves to a state key, used with state - state (object) - map of state field -> regex - stage (string) - pre or post (default: post)

Boolean composition: - all (list of match blocks) - any (list of match blocks) - not (match block or list)

docs.title: Action parameters
docs.text:
Response: - status_code (int) - content_type (string) - headers (object) - body or response_body (string)

State: - set_state (object) - key (string, template) - data (object, templated values) - ttl_seconds (int, optional)

Chaining: - chain (object) - max_hops (int, optional) - cooldown_seconds (int, optional) - ttl_seconds (int, optional)

Forwarding (server side request, not user side, to redirect / forward the user, use "Location"): - forward (object) - url (string, template) - method (string, default: POST) - headers (object, templated values) - body (string, template) - timeout_seconds (int/float, default: 5)

Create route: - create_route (object) - method (string, default: current request method) - path (string, default: current request path) - status_code (int, default: 200) - content_type (string, default: text/plain) - response_body (string, default: OK) - body_encoding (string, default: none) - headers (object)

docs.title: Templates and filters
docs.text:
Templates use {{ ... }} and can reference request context: - method, path, host, client_ip, body - headers.<name> - query.<param> - state.<field>

Filters are appended with |: - lower, upper, strip - b64encode, b64decode - url_encode, url_decode - json - json_get:<path> (dot path, supports array indexes like items.0.name)

Custom filters can be loaded from: - /root/forge/IFTTT/filters/*.py - REACH_RULE_FILTER_PATHS

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
{
  "name": "callback-rule",
  "priority": 10,
  "match": {
    "path": "/callback",
    "method": "GET",
    "stage": "post",
    "state_key": "{{query.token}}",
    "state": { "confirmed": "^yes$" }
  },
  "action": {
    "chain": { "max_hops": 3, "cooldown_seconds": 5 },
    "set_state": {
      "key": "{{query.token}}",
      "data": { "confirmed": "yes" }
    },
    "forward": {
      "url": "http://127.0.0.1:9000/ingest",
      "method": "POST",
      "headers": { "content-type": "text/plain" },
      "body": "token={{query.token}}"
    },
    "create_route": {
      "method": "GET",
      "path": "/callback",
      "response_body": "OK"
    },
    "status_code": 200,
    "body": "rule handled"
  }
}

docs.title: Logs
docs.text: REACH persists request logs to the database and exposes them via the admin API.

docs.title: Admin API
docs.text:
• GET /api/logs
  • GET /api/logs?since_id=<id>&limit=<n>&protocol=<name>
  • GET /api/logs?protocol=dns&dns_label=<label> (filter DNS by left-most label)

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
Tail logs from the admin API:

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001

Filter DNS logs for a specific operator label:

reach logs tail --protocol dns --dns-label alice

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Non-HTTP protocols (FTP/WSS/DNS) log through shared helpers.
  • DNS logs include the query type and matched zone (if any).

docs.title: REACH overview
docs.text:
REACH is a modular framework for hosting external payloads and capturing callbacks during authorized security testing. Its core is a dynamic routing and logging system backed by a database, with separate public and admin surfaces.

Pillars: - Dynamic HTTP routing for external payloads - Centralized request logging for correlation and analysis - Protocol extensions (HTTP, FTP, WSS, DNS) that feed the same logging store - Admin API for managing routes, rules, logs, and DNS zones

Typical workflow: 1) Start the core servers (public and admin). 2) Create dynamic routes or payloads. 3) Observe and correlate requests via logs. 4) Run DNS for OOB callbacks using DB-backed zones.

See the component docs for details: - servers.md - dns.md - logs.md - forge.md - config.md

docs.title: Servers and protocols
docs.text: REACH separates public traffic handling from admin control surfaces.

docs.title: Public server
docs.text:
The public server serves dynamic routes and protocol handlers. - HTTP (ASGI) - FTP (TCP capture) - WSS (WebSocket)

Start:

reach server start --role public --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

docs.title: Admin server
docs.text:
The admin server exposes CRUD APIs for routes, rules, logs, and DNS zones. Start:

reach server start --role admin --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8001

docs.title: Combined
docs.text:
Run both public and admin in one process:

reach server start --role both --port 8000

docs.title: Protocol selection
docs.text: The CLI can run multiple public protocol listeners via presets. See config.md for preset examples.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Quick checks for common setup and runtime issues.

docs.title: reach command not found
docs.text:
Install the package in your current environment:

python -m pip install -e .

docs.title: Logs tail cannot connect to admin API
docs.text:
If running --role both --port 8000, admin API is usually on 8001.

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001

docs.title: --reload fails for server start
docs.text: Auto-reload is intentionally disabled in current CLI behavior. Remove --reload.

docs.title: No DNS responses
docs.text:
Check one of these is true: - --db-zones is enabled and zones exist in DB - or --domain is set for static zone mode

If strict mode is on, only apex A/AAAA answers:

reach dns serve ... --strict-zone

docs.title: Forge route creation fails
docs.text: Validate: - admin URL is reachable (--core-url) - payload kind exists (reach forge --list) - endpoint is provided unless --dry-run is used

docs.title: Docker server start fails
docs.text: Validate: - Docker daemon is running - image can build from selected --dockerfile and --context - required ports are available on host

docs.title: DB/state issues during local dev
docs.text:
Dev utilities:

reach dev clear-logs
reach dev reset-db --yes

reset-db is destructive and should not be used in shared/prod environments.

docs.title: CLI reference
docs.text:
Top-level groups: - reach version - reach server ... - reach routes ... - reach logs ... - reach forge ... - reach dns ... - reach dev ...

Run --help on any command for full option details.

docs.title: Command guides
docs.text:
• server.md
  • routes.md
  • logs.md
  • forge.md
  • dns.md
  • dev.md

docs.title: Version commands
docs.text:
Show runtime + component versions:

reach version

Show a single component:

reach version --component ui

docs.title: reach dev
docs.text: Developer utilities for local environments.

docs.title: Clear in-memory request logs
docs.text: reach dev clear-logs

docs.title: Reset database schema
docs.text:
reach dev reset-db --yes

reset-db drops and recreates all tables. Treat as destructive.

docs.title: reach dns
docs.text: Run DNS service for OOB callbacks and query logging.

docs.title: Start with DB-backed zones
docs.text: reach dns serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 53 --db-zones

docs.title: Start with static single zone
docs.text: reach dns serve --domain oob.example.com --a 203.0.113.10

docs.title: Useful options
docs.text:
• --strict-zone disable wildcard subdomain answering
  • --zones-refresh 2.0 DB zone refresh interval in seconds
  • --tcp also listen on TCP
  • --daemon --pidfile /tmp/reach-dns.pid run background service (POSIX)
  • --async-logging reduce response blocking under heavy query load

For zone CRUD APIs and behavior details, see ../dns.md.

docs.title: reach forge
docs.text: Generate payloads and register routes through the admin API.

docs.title: List payload kinds
docs.text:
reach forge --list

Describe one kind:

reach forge --list --kind xss_basic

docs.title: Generate payload and create route
docs.text:
reach forge payload new xss_basic \
  --endpoint /xss \
  --payload-kwarg callback_url=http://127.0.0.1:8000/beacon \
  --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001

docs.title: Dry run (no route creation)
docs.text:
reach forge payload new xss_basic \
  --dry-run \
  --payload-kwarg callback_url=http://127.0.0.1:8000/beacon

docs.title: Useful options
docs.text:
• --header KEY=VALUE set response headers (repeatable)
  • --server-header-file <path> pick Server header values from file
  • --method, --status, --content-type tune route response metadata

See ../forge.md for plugin-level behavior.

docs.title: reach logs
docs.text: Stream request logs from REACH admin API.

docs.title: Tail logs continuously
docs.text: reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001

docs.title: Fetch once
docs.text: reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001 --once

docs.title: Filter examples
docs.text:
By protocol:

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001 --protocol dns

By DNS label:

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001 --dns-label alice

By regex:

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001 --regex "callback|token="

Include request headers:

reach logs tail --core-url http://127.0.0.1:8001 --header

docs.title: reach routes
docs.text: Inspect static and dynamic routes.

docs.title: List all routes
docs.text: reach routes list

docs.title: List only static framework routes
docs.text: reach routes static

docs.title: List only dynamic DB-backed routes
docs.text: reach routes dynamic

docs.title: Show payload bodies
docs.text:
reach routes dynamic --show-body
reach routes dynamic --full-body

Dynamic route commands support --decode/--raw for base64 payload display.

docs.title: reach server
docs.text: Start and manage REACH Core services.

docs.title: Inspect supported protocols
docs.text: reach server protocols

docs.title: Start public server
docs.text: reach server start --role public --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

docs.title: Start admin server
docs.text: reach server start --role admin --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8001

docs.title: Start both public and admin
docs.text:
reach server start --role both --port 8000

In both mode, admin defaults to --port + 1 unless overridden.

docs.title: Useful options
docs.text:
• --preset <file> load JSON config
  • --port-public and --port-admin split listeners explicitly
  • --protocol http|ftp|wss choose public protocol for single-protocol mode
  • --docker run through Docker from CLI

For configuration precedence and preset structure, see ../config.md.
